text:
Guys we need to talk about $AAOI...

$AAOI is one of the most controversial stocks in the world and I enjoy the disagreement.

Pretty cool that's it up 3x since I went long with many thousands of investors riding it with me up some portion of that gain on @joinautopilot.

But what's cooler is that I think it's still asymmetrical here.

So let's break it down and figure out where we're at $170/share today.

Management argues they can get to $471M in monthly transceiver revenue by Q2 2027.

If you're new to photonics, this is because transceivers are one of the hottest commodities in data centers right now (they translate electricity to light) and 3 hyperscalers are working with $AAOI on this.

At management's 40% gross margin target, that's ~$5.6B in annualized revenue. Run past opex, interest, tax, dilution, we might land around $14-15 in EPS on that run rate.

That's a 2028 number though because 2027 is the ramp mid year, so let's call it bull case $9 of EPS in 2027.

So at $170/share right now, on these calcs, you're paying 19x 2027 earnings, falling dramatically to 12x for 2028.

Don't trust management? Fair. Haircut both the revenue and the margin target by 30%, let's say we get to $4B at a 28% gross margin instead of $5.6B at 40%.

You still land near ~$6 in EPS, just over the analyst mean estimate for 2027E. That's 28x at $170, still WAY cheaper than $LITE and $COHR trade right now.

$LITE is at a 50x NTM forward PE per Fiscal AI.
$COHR is at 47x NTM forward PE per Fiscal AI.

You're paying a fraction of the multiples of its peer group for a faster growing business because execution is still a huge question mark.

If you are an asymmetric trader like me, you might have a large concentrated position in $AAOI for these reasons.

I do believe the stock at $170 is still a solid entry point and I began loading again heavily in the 160's.

$AAOI Up 13% AH 

It is the largest position in my photonics fund.

- Q4 revenue: $134.3M (+34% YoY) 
- Gross margin: 31.2% vs 28.7% last year  
- $455.7M. That's double 2024's $249.4M. 
- Q1 2026 guide is $150M–$165M. Margins holding.

Photonics is next.

text:
The restrictions on Anthropics Fable 5 and Mythos 5 aren’t bearish

If anything, they’re proof that AI has become strategic national infrastructure
Governments don’t restrict technologies they view as unimportant

The bigger takeaway is what happens next 

The UAE, France etc and others will accelerate sovereign AI initiatives
More domestic compute, more local models = data centers
No country wants to depend on infrastructure that can be switched off by another jurisdiction overnight

That means more demand across the entire AI supply chain

Compute. Chips. Lasers. Fiber. Power. Cooling.

The picks and shovels providers don’t care whose flag is flying over the data center

There may be some short term volatility, but long term this looks like another tailwind for the infrastructure layer

The AI buildout isn’t slowing down. It’s becoming more fragmented, more localized, and ultimately larger

The real bottlenecks remain the same
lasers, InP substrates, silicon photonics, power delivery, and fiber connectivity

Compute: $NVDA $AMD $AVGO

Optics: $AAOI $SIVE $COHR $LITE $AXTI $AIXA $SOI $FOCI $TSEM $NCI

Power: $NVTS $AOSL $POWI

Sovereign Compute/DC: $NBIS $GLW

More sovereign clusters means more demand chasing the same constrained supply chain

The supercycle remains intact

I have positions in many of these names. NFA.

text:
The most common question that I've gotten on this post is that all of $AXTI, $LITE, $AIXA, $COHR, $SOI, $TSEM have gone parabolic so why is this even an interesting space anymore?

My spicy take is that I LIKE stocks whose charts are a little parabolic (of course only to an extent)

What does a parabolic chart mean? It means that the shares were trading hands at a lower price in the past. Buyers were buying from sellers and sellers were selling to buyers. They were wrong about the value of the company. The next day they buy and sell at a slightly higher price. They are still wrong. and so on and so on. That is how you get a parabolic chart.

If the market is in a constant state of being wrong, what is the probability that they've stopped being wrong today? I believe it's actually lower especially in industries with multiple overlapping secular tailwinds like optics (photonics + networking + AI).

text:
Jensen Huang told a room of global investors that AI is not one industry. It is five stacked on top of each other. Most people are investing in layer four and ignoring layers one through three entirely.

He called it the five-layer cake.

Layer one is energy. Jensen said this is the single greatest opportunity for the energy industry in a hundred years.

The first time in a century that the grid in most countries can actually attract serious capital. Nuclear, solar, wind, hydrogen, it does not matter what form. If it produces energy, it gets funded. Siemens, GE Vernova, Mitsubishi. That is why they are all doing so well right now.

Layer two is chips, computers, networking, and silicon photonics. Everything that processes the intelligence.

Layer three is infrastructure. Land, power, buildings, data center operations. Every single one in short supply today.

Layer four is the model layer. OpenAI, Anthropic. The layer everyone talks about.

Layer five is applications. Every startup applying AI to financial services, legal, healthcare, logistics, transportation. Last year alone, a hundred billion dollars of venture capital went into this layer. The single largest VC year in the history of humanity.

Then he said the number that stopped me cold.

We are putting one trillion dollars into this five-layer cake this year. That sounds enormous. Jensen thinks the AI industry will eventually run at twenty trillion dollars per year.

We are one trillion in of a twenty trillion dollar per year ecosystem.

Most people watching AI are staring at layer four. Jensen was describing layers one through five as a single compounding system where every layer feeds the one above it.

The people who understand that will invest differently than the people who do not.

"those matrix multiplications, the base of the AI, will use a lot of memory don't you think? Let's buy Memory stocks...." 

Then $MU $SNDK etc. went to Saturn.

Now AI Agents can be the "matrix multiplication" equivalents for Networking:

"A single agentic task can generate 450% more network traffic than a human doing the same work" 👀🔥🔥

Imagine the networking traffic 🚀 And in AI, Networking ==> Photonics & Glass $LITE $AAOI $NOK  $GLW

A single agentic task can generate 450% more network traffic than a human doing the same work. Roughly 70% of that traffic is inference. Nearly 10% of AI flows now carry more data upstream than downstream, versus 0.5% for typical web traffic. And Cisco says enterprise traffic could grow 9x by 2035 with agentic AI adoption, versus 2.5x without it. That ties directly into something I wrote about recently: Scale-across. AI clusters are getting too large to keep in one place. At a certain point, the constraint becomes physical: Power. Land. Cooling. Permitting. Fiber access. Route availability. So instead of forcing everything into one giant site, hyperscalers start connecting multiple buildings, campuses, and facilities together so they can operate like one larger AI environment. Cisco’s report adds the usage layer. Agentic AI turns one request into a chain: Prompt → planner → model call → tool call → retrieval → more model calls → action. That creates more: > traffic > upstream flow. > machine-to-machine exchange > persistent context movement > pressure on the infrastructure underneath the model. The first pressure shows up closest to compute. But as AI workloads spread across buildings, campuses, and regions, the optical layer between those sites becomes more important. That is the scale-across trade. Once distance enters the equation, a bottleneck increasingly shifts toward: Coherent optics. Fiber. DCI. Dark fiber routes. Pump lasers. Narrow-linewidth lasers. Optical amplification. Indium phosphide capacity. That is why this keeps showing up across optical earnings calls. $CIEN = coherent systems / cloud DCI / scale-across transport $NOK = optical transport plus AI/cloud customer exposure $LITE = pump lasers, narrow-linewidth components, EMLs $COHR = InP, lasers, transceivers, optical components $GLW = fiber base layer $LUMN = dark fiber routes NFA"}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2062396054449144251"}}, "rest_id": "2067301749283766756", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "22169", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2067347493356966120", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3979", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
20260617 Market Review
• I don't to talk to you about the FOMC today. You can get that from tons of other website, talking heads etc. Most of these have no value add and not actionable. What's important is HOW do we interpret what is going and HOW do we position going forward? ie HOW DO WE THINK AHEAD OF THE CROWD?
• Market breadth ended up being quite weak, with the VIX threatening to go back above 19 (a line in the sand I watch). But what really caught my eye was the USD. It really took off after the FOMC and stopped right at resistance. If this leads to a sustained breakout to the upside, it may not be friendly to markets. Watching that 100.50 level closely. With the USD rallying, it is no wonder $GOLD would get hit. Another lower high forming. 
• Indices seem to be stuck in chop for now. AI seems to be the only game in town but I wanna dig deeper into the various subsectors today. 
• Semiconductors. Ok but hitting resistance. 
$NVDA losing the 50SMA today is not a good look. 
• Semicon Equipment and Materials. Big list in here, very mixed picture. Nothing I would buy in a hurry.
• Racks: $Dell ok but $HPE selling off. Neutral because the whole group is not looking uniform. 
• Photonics: $LITE is the group leader and it has been mucking around the 50SMA. Not healthy. Neither is $AAOI, $GLW or $AXTI. 
• Memory: $STX and $WDC still making new highs, but $MU hitting resistance. $SNDK still king. Of all the AI Hardware playes, Memory is probably the only subsector which I think overall is still healthy. But even that is extended. 
• So the above are the AI Leading Sectors that I pay attention to and overall, my conclusion is that the easy trade in AI Hardware is over. It can still go higher, but unless it sets up for me properly (with a pullback or an orderly sideways congestion for a few weeks to a few months), I will find it hard to bet big on them for now. 
• I am still very bullish on the AI trade but if Hardware is not the game for now, what then?
• Next we have the Neoclouds. $IREN $CRWV $NBIS etc. $NBIS is the clear leader. Generally ok and I would still look for opportunities in them.
• Software. I was hopeful that it might have pulled back too much and would find support but that didn't eventuate. In line with my trading philosophy, "if it ain't working, it ain't staying". I will continue to stalk this sector as they are going to be the "operational" layer of AI and I think there will be several companies in there that will benefit. 
• Cybersecurity is one software sub-sector I am super keen on and have taken a position accordingly. Look at $CRWD and $PANW (both we took in our port today) for example. They are holding up like a champ and tightening up really nicely. This is an extremely healthy consolidation after that fierce run they had since early May, and the best part is, they are NOT giving back much of the gains! THIS is a sign of a Market Leader. Can we be wrong or too early? Of course, but then that’s why we have stops to protect us. 
• Biotech. This is the sector that I think will benefit greatly from AI (check out the $NVDA x $LLY presentation on the NVDA website). I have been stalking XBI for a few weeks now and we finally broke higher today. Great volume too. 
• Fintech is another interesting subsector that is showing a bit of relative strength recently. Worth keeping an eye on. Eg $DAVE $AFRM etc.
• In conclusion, I think the overall market might be in for a bit of digestion. $MAGS  is pulling back hard (not good). The AI HARDWARE trade is no longer easy. But the AI USAGE trade I think is just starting. Biotech and Cybersecurity $CIBR are the standouts to me and we are positioning accordingly. Software $IGV is a maybe, keep stalking.
• All the best.

You can get that from tons of other website, talking heads etc. Most of these have no value add and not actionable. What's important is HOW do we interpret what is going and HOW do we position going forward? ie HOW DO WE THINK AHEAD OF THE CROWD? • Market breadth ended up being quite weak, with the VIX threatening to go back above 19 (a line in the sand I watch). But what really caught my eye was the USD. It really took off after the FOMC and stopped right at resistance. If this leads to a sustained breakout to the upside, it may not be friendly to markets. Watching that 100.50 level closely. With the USD rallying, it is no wonder $GOLD would get hit. Another lower high forming. • Indices seem to be stuck in chop for now. AI seems to be the only game in town but I wanna dig deeper into the various subsectors today. • Semiconductors. Ok but hitting resistance. $NVDA losing the 50SMA today is not a good look. • Semicon Equipment and Materials. Big list in here, very mixed picture. Nothing I would buy in a hurry. • Racks: $Dell ok but $HPE selling off. Neutral because the whole group is not looking uniform. • Photonics: $LITE is the group leader and it has been mucking around the 50SMA. Not healthy. Neither is $AAOI, $GLW or $AXTI. • Memory: $STX and $WDC still making new highs, but $MU hitting resistance. $SNDK still king. Of all the AI Hardware playes, Memory is probably the only subsector which I think overall is still healthy. But even that is extended. • So the above are the AI Leading Sectors that I pay attention to and overall, my conclusion is that the easy trade in AI Hardware is over. It can still go higher, but unless it sets up for me properly (with a pullback or an orderly sideways congestion for a few weeks to a few months), I will find it hard to bet big on them for now. • I am still very bullish on the AI trade but if Hardware is not the game for now, what then? • Next we have the Neoclouds. $IREN $CRWV $NBIS etc. $NBIS is the clear leader. Generally ok and I would still look for opportunities in them. • Software. I was hopeful that it might have pulled back too much and would find support but that didn't eventuate. In line with my trading philosophy, "if it ain't working, it ain't staying". I will continue to stalk this sector as they are going to be the "operational" layer of AI and I think there will be several companies in there that will benefit. • Cybersecurity is one software sub-sector I am super keen on and have taken a position accordingly. Look at $CRWD and $PANW (both we took in our port today) for example. They are holding up like a champ and tightening up really nicely. This is an extremely healthy consolidation after that fierce run they had since early May, and the best part is, they are NOT giving back much of the gains! THIS is a sign of a Market Leader. Can we be wrong or too early? Of course, but then that’s why we have stops to protect us. • Biotech. This is the sector that I think will benefit greatly from AI (check out the $NVDA x $LLY presentation on the NVDA website). I have been stalking XBI for a few weeks now and we finally broke higher today. Great volume too. • Fintech is another interesting subsector that is showing a bit of relative strength recently. Worth keeping an eye on. Eg $DAVE $AFRM etc. • In conclusion, I think the overall market might be in for a bit of digestion. $MAGS is pulling back hard (not good). The AI HARDWARE trade is no longer easy. But the AI USAGE trade I think is just starting. Biotech and Cybersecurity $CIBR are the standouts to me and we are positioning accordingly. Software $IGV is a maybe, keep stalking. • All the best."}}}, "rest_id": "2067347221503471760", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "187", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Cleanest read of the quarter, and agreed it's a genuine, gimmick-free beat. The one thing I'd push on: this is a high-quality revenue print, not yet a high-quality margin one. Intelligent Infrastructure is carrying the AI growth (21%, guided +32% into Q4) but sits at 6.1% core margin, only ~30bps above the 5.8% corporate blend. So the AI surge is roughly margin-neutral to the company today. The whole re-rate toward 6%+ is a within-II mix story (photonics, cooling, power, Hanley) that management itself calls gradual. Volume is here now; the accretive mix is FY27's job. That's the gap between "beat-and-raise" and "re-rate," and it's why a 10bps full-year margin lift on a $1B revenue raise is the number to sit with.

That's the gap between \"beat-and-raise\" and \"re-rate,\" and it's why a 10bps full-year margin lift on a $1B revenue raise is the number to sit with."}}}, "rest_id": "2067339603770782182", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$SIVE has had a tremendous run, but at current levels it looks increasingly priced for perfection.

Meanwhile, $LBIRD remains largely overlooked despite being directly exposed to one of the fastest-growing trends in aerospace: satellite constellations and optical communications.

As SpaceX, Starlink and the broader space economy continue to expand, Lumibird's laser and photonics expertise could become significantly more valuable.

Sometimes the best opportunities are where the market isn't looking yet. #SpaceX #Starlink #Photonics #Space

text:
The best-performing thing in the AI trade this year is not the GPU, the model, or the memory
-> it is a wafer most investors have never heard of, can't pronounce, and would struggle to explain. It ran over 1000% in one of my books. Here is what it actually is.

It is the substrate. 
The crystal wafer a laser gets grown on, sitting four layers upstream of the chip everyone watches. And the reason I trust the signal is that it did not turn up in one book. It turned up in two.

I run a US bottleneck book and a separate EU one. No shared holdings, different construction, different markets. This year the strongest layer in both was the same one almost nobody can name. Not the accelerator. Not even the laser. The wafer the laser is grown on.

Look at the leaderboards side by side.

In the US book, InP and Substrates is the second-strongest basket of the year, behind only memory. In the EU book, InP and Substrates is also second, behind only photonics. Same layer, same rank, two markets that do not coordinate.

The names tell it cleaner:

- US -> $AXTI +475% YTD
- EU -> $IQE.L +1018%, $SOI.PA (Soitec) +438%
and $AIXA.DE (AIXTRON) +247% selling the reactors that grow the wafers

The mechanism is the photonics build. Co-packaged optics needs lasers. Lasers are III-V material, indium phosphide and gallium arsenide, not plain silicon. III-V starts as an epitaxial wafer grown on a specialty substrate, and that supply is small, slow, and hard to add. When optical demand turned from slideware into orders, the squeeze hit the substrate first. Both books found the same constrained object without talking to each other.

Worth the honesty: these are thin floats, so the moves are violent and part momentum. Strip the two biggest and the picture cools. But the substrate is the real upstream gate on the optics bottleneck everyone keeps drawing one layer too high.

The feed watches the photonic switch. The squeeze is one floor below it, in the wafer that switch can't exist without.

text:
⚛️ Atom Computing and Nu Quantum have signed an MoU to integrate neutral-atom quantum computers with state-of-the-art photonic networking hardware.

🤝 This collaboration is set to accelerate the path to utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computing systems.

👀 By focusing on integrated photonics switches and distributed architectures, they're addressing key challenges on the road to real-world quantum utility.

🚀 Excited to see where this partnership leads the industry!

Get it here 👉 https://t.co/QcBJzJz04F

#QuantumComputing #AtomComputing #NuQuantum #QuantumNetworking #Science #QuantumScience @Atom_Computing @NuQuantumLtd

text:
Most people see $NOK as sleepy European telecom. They’re missing what it bought.

Less than 2% of global semiconductor advanced test & packaging happens in the US. Nokia owns one of the only domestic sites packaging photonic chips into optical modules for AI data centers — and it didn’t come from telecom. It came from Infinera, absorbed Feb 2025.

Now they’re scaling it with CHIPS money behind them:

~$30M Nokia capex + ~$10M federal CHIPS tax credit + ~$4M PA aid. 500+ jobs. Part of a $4B US commitment.

This isn’t a photonics rocket — Allentown is a small piece of a $20B+ company. But that’s the point. You’re getting a CHIPS-backed domestic photonics packaging franchise inside a name the market still prices as legacy telecom.

A misread legacy company with a real foothold in the AI photonics layer. The choke point before the crowd sees it.

Bullish $NOK.

Estimated supplier revenue per $130k switch (based on BoM values):
NVIDIA (Switch ASICs + Optical Engines design): ~$12k (ASICs) + significant share of engines → major portion.

TSMC (Fabrication/Packaging of ASICs & engines): Substantial share of ~$44.4k combined ASIC/engines value.

Corning (FAUs + SM Fiber): ~$3.6k + $12.3k = ~$15.9k.

Lumentum / Coherent / Sumitomo (ELS + CW Lasers): ~$7.2k.

Senko / others (MPO connectors): ~$5.8k.

Additional (Shuffle Box, assembly): Balance to total BoM ~$75.8k.

text:
In my opinion these are the best positioned companies in their respective niches. 

Sectors covered : AI Infrastructure, Memory, Optical Transceivers, Compound Semi, Specialty Foundry, Power Semi, Laser Systems, Autonomous Defense, Quantum. (NFA)

$CIFR - industrial scale HPC data centers on 15 year hyperscaler leases. $11.4B contracted backlog.
They build and own data centers where AI companies park their supercomputers.

$SK Hynix : dominant HBM3E and HBM4 supplier to NVIDIA, DRAM and NAND flash. Gross margin 68%. Revenue +198% YoY. Forward PE: 6.7x. 
Makes the ultra-fast memory stacked directly on top of AI chips, without it the GPU does absolutely nothing.

$AAOI : vertically integrated optical transceiver manufacturer. Makes its own lasers, chips and optics for hyperscaler data center networking in-house.

$IQE - epitaxial wafer supplier, grows the compound semiconductor crystal layers that photonics, RF and power chips are built on. Multi-year supply deals with MACOM and Tower Semiconductor.
The special wafers everyone else builds their laser and signal chips on top of.

$XFAB - specialty analog and mixed-signal foundry for automotive, medical and industrial markets. P/S: 1.69x vs peers at 3–5x.
The factory that makes the unglamorous but completely essential chips running in every car, MRI machine and factory floor.

$AOSL - power semiconductor designer and manufacturer. MOSFETs and power ICs for AI data centers, EVs and consumer electronics. 
The chips that control how electricity flows inside every device you own.

$LPKF - precision laser systems for PCB manufacturing, EV battery cell welding and solar panel processing.
The laser machines that drill, cut and weld the boards and batteries inside almost every electronic device and electric car.

$ONDS - autonomous drone systems, rail automation and defense robotics. $1.47B cash on balance sheet. 32% of float is short.
Robots and drones replacing humans in the most dangerous, repetitive and strategically important jobs.

$INFQ - neutral atom quantum computing, sensing and atomic clocks for national security and AI computation. $443M cash on a $3.25B market cap.
Next generation computers built on individual atoms instead of silicon.

text:
After too many hours of research/DD to count, these are my Top 5 AI Stocks in the Top 5 AI Sectors to Invest in:

1. Neoclouds: The GPU & Compute landlords. Whoever owns the compute owns what every AI company on the earth needs to grow & succeed.

$NBIS - Nebius. Spun out of Yandex, rebuilt as a pure-play AI cloud. Microsoft and Meta committed billions in contracted backlog. The fastest growing neocloud on the board. My favorite pick as a long-term investment.

$CRWV - CoreWeave. The American-founded neocloud. SemiAnalysis rated this Neocloud in a class of it's own, which can not be understated.  Deals with Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta, Perplexity, Google, Microsoft... the list goes on forever. 

$HUT -  Hut 8. Started as a bitcoin miner, pivoted hard into AI hosting and power infrastructure. Owns the power, not just the GPUs. They're landing deals left and right, definitely an underappreciated sleeper pick.

$APLD - Applied Digital. Same playbook. Former crypto miner now building purpose-designed AI data center campuses with long-term hyperscaler leases.

$CIFR - data center host for Anthropic's directly, purchased Google TPU v7 Ironwoods (400K units, ~$10B), backed by a 10-year, $3B+ Fluidstack hosting deal where Google guarantees $1.4B of lease obligations for a 5.4% equity stake. One of the only neocloud-adjacent names with real exposure to Google's silicon instead of pure Nvidia GPU rental.

2. Optical / Photonics:

Because every GPU is useless if it can't talk to the GPU next to it.

$CRDO - Credo. The connectivity layer inside every AI cluster. Active electrical cables and DSPs that scale with every rack hyperscalers deploy.

$LITE - Lumentum. Legacy telecom optics company that's become a core 800G/1.6T transceiver supplier for the AI buildout.

$ALAB - Astera Labs. Connectivity chips that solve the bottleneck between GPUs, memory, and storage. Pure-play AI infrastructure with almost no legacy drag.

$COHR - Coherent. Lasers, optical components, and transceivers spanning the entire photonics stack from datacom to industrial.

$MRVL - Marvell. Custom silicon and photonic fabric for hyperscalers. The company quietly inside more AI racks than people realize.

3. Memory:

The most cyclical, most violently mispriced sector in semis. HBM demand changed the entire setup.

$DRAM - DRAM ETF. One ticker, every important memory company on the planet, including South Korean companies like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronic, companies you CAN'T INVEST IN with most brokerages.

$SNDK - SanDisk. Spun off from Western Digital, now a pure-play NAND flash story riding the same supply tightness as everyone else in this sector.

$MU - Micron. One of three companies on Earth that makes HBM. The clearest direct line from AI buildout to memory revenue.

$WDC - Western Digital. Hard drive and enterprise storage demand riding the same data center capex wave as everything else on this list.

$STX - Seagate. The other half of the storage duopoly. Enterprise nearline drives are seeing the same supply crunch dynamics as memory.

4. Analog / Power Semis:

Unsexy. Necessary. Every data center, every EV, every robot needs power management silicon that doesn't get the AI premium yet:

$MXL - MaxLinear. Smaller-cap analog and mixed-signal play with infrastructure and data center exposure that's still flying under the radar.

$STM - STMicroelectronics. European chip giant spanning auto, industrial, and power semis. Way out of favor relative to its diversification.

$ON - ON Semiconductor. Power semis for EVs and industrial, now leaning harder into data center power delivery as a growth vector.

$VSH - Vishay. Passive components: resistors, capacitors, diodes. Boring until you realize literally everything electronic needs them.

$POWI - Power Integrations. High-voltage power conversion chips. Small cap, niche, and positioned for the power efficiency problem AI data centers haven't solved yet.

5. Physical AI / Robotics:

It's coming, soon, and the market is beginning to realize it.

$OUST - Ouster. Lidar for robotics, industrial, and autonomy. Consolidated the space after merging with Velodyne, now the survivor.

$VICR - Vicor. High-density power modules for robotics, AI servers, and defense. Power delivery at the component level, not the rack level.

$VPG - Vishay Precision Group. Precision sensors and strain gauges. The torque and force-sensing hardware that gives robots a sense of touch.

$AEVA - Aeva. 4D lidar with built-in velocity sensing. Smaller and earlier stage than the rest of this list, highest risk, highest ceiling.

$AMBA - Ambarella. Edge AI vision chips. Powers the cameras and perception systems inside cars, robots, and security infrastructure.

I believe a portfolio with these 25 names will severely outperform the market over the next few years. I have 7 figures throughout many of these names. These are all names I'm currently invested in, or plan on investing in in the near future.

None of this is financial advice.

text:
Everyone talks about AI chips.

Almost nobody talks about what actually limits them.

Imagine a Formula 1 car.

Now imagine fueling it through a drinking straw.

That’s modern AI.

The problem isn’t computing power anymore.

It’s moving data.

For decades, the semiconductor industry got faster by making transistors smaller.

But physics eventually showed up.

You can’t keep building bigger chips forever:
• Larger chips are harder to manufacture.
• More defects = lower yields.
• Costs explode.

So the industry found a workaround:

➡️ Build many small chips.
➡️ Place them extremely close together.
➡️ Connect them so tightly they behave like one giant chip.

This is called Advanced Packaging.

And it’s becoming just as important as the chip itself.

Then came HBM (High Bandwidth Memory):

➡️ Memory is placed right next to the AI processor.
➡️ Data travels millimeters instead of centimeters.
➡️ Massive speed gains.
➡️ Lower power consumption.

Without HBM and advanced packaging, modern AI wouldn’t exist.

The next step?

Photonics.

Instead of moving data with electrons, move it with light.

Less power.
More bandwidth.
Longer distances.

The AI infrastructure stack is becoming:

Transistors → Packaging → HBM → Photonics

Potential winners across the stack:

• NVIDIA — AI compute
• AMD — AI compute
• TSMC — advanced packaging leader
• BESI — advanced packaging equipment
• ASMPT — packaging equipment
• Amkor — packaging services
• SK Hynix — HBM leader
• Micron — HBM
• Samsung — HBM
• Ayar Labs — optical interconnects
• Broadcom — networking & optics
• Marvell — optical networking
• Sivers Semiconductors — lasers & photonics
• Coherent — optical components
• Lumentum — optical components

The market spent 30 years optimizing computation.

The next 30 years may be about optimizing the movement of data.

From first principles: The whole semiconductor industry runs on one engine: Moore's Law. Every couple of years, you could shrink transistors, pack 2x as many onto a chip...and get more compute for the same cost. But the cost of moving a bit of data from one place to another doesn't improve at the same rate as the computation gains. Mainly because moving electrons vast distances costs time/energy. And going off-chip and into another package is super expensive. And ofc you can't just build one giant chips because yields fall exponentially w/ area. Even if you 2x chip size, you can lose most of the yield. This is where you stop thinking about the chip itself, but more about the package. Advanced packaging. Very simply: If you can't make one big chip, make loads of smaller ones + connect them so tightly that they act like one big chip. You get the benefits of a giant chip without paying the yield penalty. By decreasing power consumption + increasing speed. And today, bandwidth is probably the defining problem for AI accelerators especially. Advanced packaging provides that bandwidth by putting HBM next to the logic + wiring it together with a higher volume of shorter/denser connections. You literally can't route enough wires across a circuit board to feed a modern GPU cos the bandwidth those chips need only exists if the memory is packaged right up against the compute. There's ofc more depth to this like specific advanced packaging components or technologies. Including HBM's role in the whole thing and stuff like glass substrates too. But that's a short story if anyone wanted to learn a bit more rather than getting a bunch of random ticker symbols. But fwiw, my favourite pure-play advanced packaging equipment name is $BESI that I hold. text:
Everyone thinks quantum computing is about advanced theoretical physics. 

I analyzed 350+ quantum job listings and the reality is much different.

It turns out you are basically building fridges and writing control software.

Combined Skill Demand (% of listings asking for it):

1/ Dominant:
- Python ~50% 
- Qiskit ~50% 
- Quantum Control Systems ~50%
- Cryogenics ~45% 
- FPGA Programming ~35% 
- Quantum Error Correction ~35% 
- Fault-Tolerant Computing ~35% 

2/ Common:
- Superconducting Qubits ~25% 
- PyTorch ~20% 
- Microwave Engineering ~15% 
- Logical Qubits ~15% 
- Dilution Refrigerators ~15% 
- Photonics ~12% 

3/ Niche 
- Post-Quantum Cryptography ~10% 
- Surface Codes ~8% 
- Photonic Qubits ~8% 
 - Trapped Ions ~7% 
- Process Integration / SPC ~6% 
- Semiconductor ~5% 
- Quantum Phase Estimation ~5% 
- OpenQASM ~5% 
- PennyLane ~3% 

Bottom line: 3 things most quantum roles want

1. Python + Qiskit (~50%)
2. Quantum Control Systems (~50%)
3. Cryogenics (~45%)

text:
Q1 2026 was a bumper quarter in venture capital. Investors deployed $300 billion of fresh capital, more than double the previous quarter and surpassing 70% of all venture spending in 2025.

The devil is, of course, in the detail: $188 billion of that cash went to four companies – OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Waymo – accounting for 60% of all venture capital deployed in Q1.

VCs generally revel in change, but even by our standards what it takes to raise has shifted at an unprecedented rate over the past 12 months: expectations of early-stage founders – in terms of defensibility, scaling, and pricing – have been completely rewritten.

Fearing the ‘AI-pocalypse’, early-stage founders could be forgiven for feeling despondent seeing these figures. The outlook looks daunting, but it doesn’t need to be. The concentration of capital around a handful of AI giants is changing the rules, but the game goes on. The question for founders right now is not whether Big AI wins, but how can smaller companies survive and thrive alongside it.

A Rising Tide Is Lifting Most Boats

Investment in the US rose 190% YoY in Q1 but deal count fell 26%, meaning that capital is concentrating into fewer, larger cheques. However, the remaining $112 billion of funding sits in line with recent quarterly highs, and the environment for smaller rounds remains robust.

Frontier lab mega-rounds do not directly compete with early-stage cheques: indeed, pre-Seed to Series A companies are growing faster than ever before. Stripe recently revealed data showing that the top 100 AI-native companies are scaling from $1 million to $30 million ARR five times faster than previous software generations.

It’s still a very good time to be an early-stage AI company, especially one that can move quickly and provide genuine agentic solutions rather than simple GPT wrappers. That distinction matters because the startups most under threat are those with no meaningful moat – i.e. businesses that lack sufficient brand, switching costs or economies of scale. These companies will be squeezed not only by platform owners from above them, but also by falling prices below them.

Learnings from the SaaSpocalypse

It wasn’t so long ago that Anthropic’s Claude Cowork launched and within hours wiped hundreds of millions off the valuations of some of the world’s leading software companies. Investors and founders alike feared that a new army of AI agents was about to eat everybody’s lunch.

As of June 2026, however, debate on the death of SaaS is ongoing. Enterprise customers could theoretically vibe code much of their own software, but very few are actually going to do so. At least not yet. At the same time, the opposing view – that concerns about model companies are entirely overblown – has become equally unhelpful. The phrase “what if model companies do this?” has become a catch-all objection that often substitutes for harder conversations around business fundamentals.

The right question is not whether model companies will kill software. They won’t. It’s which software companies will become stronger as models improve, and which will become increasingly commoditised by them. That distinction matters because the bar to raise has doubled while investor attention has narrowed dramatically. Raising money outside AI is a tough ask in 2026, but that doesn’t mean every company needs to become an AI company. It means founders need to ask harder questions.

If OpenAI or Anthropic launched this feature tomorrow, what would still belong to us? Are we building a product, or are we building an advantage? Do we control proprietary data, hardware, scientific know-how, distribution, or regulation? Does our business become stronger as models improve, or weaker? Would customers still choose us if intelligence itself became abundant and cheap? These are the questions investors are asking too.

The Best Offense Is a Good Defense

Whatever you do as an early-stage founder right now, defense is key.

Investors are gravitating towards sectors like robotics, defence, photonics, biotech, and novel compute. This isn’t because they are fashionable, but because they possess characteristics that are difficult for foundation model providers to replicate.

Robotics and defence require integration with the physical world, complex systems, and long customer relationships. Photonics and novel compute rely on years of scientific research and manufacturing expertise. Biotech combines proprietary data, regulatory barriers, and deep domain knowledge. Intelligence alone is not enough. 

Investors are looking for companies with assets that cannot simply be replaced by agents. Proprietary data, specialised hardware, scientific expertise and difficult-to-reproduce infrastructure are becoming durable moats. 

No matter what, we are in for a turbulent remainder of 2026. Capital is concentrating at every level, the revenue bar to raise has roughly doubled in five years, and the companies getting funded look nothing like they did three years ago.

This looks bad for early-stage founders, but it doesn’t need to be. Every technological wave creates new giants, but it also creates new opportunities for smaller players that understand where defensibility lies. For founders, the message is simple but not easy: assume intelligence becomes abundant and build the things that remain scarce.

No matter what, we are in for a turbulent remainder of 2026. Capital is concentrating at every level, the revenue bar to raise has roughly doubled in five years, and the companies getting funded look nothing like they did three years ago.

This looks bad for early-stage founders, but it doesn’t need to be. Every technological wave creates new giants, but it also creates new opportunities for smaller players that understand where defensibility lies. For founders, the message is simple but not easy: assume intelligence becomes abundant and build the things that remain scarce.

The opinions expressed in https://t.co/opgY7EYkBs commentary pieces are solely the views of their authors and do not necessarily reflect the opinions and beliefs of Fortune.

text:
Some thoughts on $AXTI…

As I work through the China AI optics supply ramp, the AXT setup keeps getting more complicated.

China is reportedly preparing a ~$295B five-year AI data-center buildout plan.

At the same time, the domestic optics stack is ramping everywhere:

> Source Photonics / Dongshan is expanding optical chips and modules.

> Chinese suppliers are pushing EMLs, CW lasers, InP PICs, silicon photonics and optical engines.

> Yunnan Germanium, Guangdong Xiandao and AXT / Tongmei are expanding InP substrate output.

> Innolight and Eoptolink continue scaling data-center transceivers.

So on one hand, this is clearly positive for AXT’s China-based demand. Domestic China demand is cleaner because the export-permit issue really shows up when substrates leave China.

If China’s AI buildout absorbs more domestic optical supply, AXT could be sitting in the middle of a very important local bottleneck.

But there is another side.

More domestic InP substrate capacity from Yunnan Germanium, Guangdong Xiandao and others could reduce AXT’s pricing power over time if those suppliers scale and qualify.

And the geopolitical angle cuts both ways.

China has real leverage here. If it wanted to slow U.S. substrate availability, it does not need to fully ban exports. It can continue to slow permits, condition approvals, request more end-user data, or prioritize domestic demand.

That would be supportive for China’s own AI optics ecosystem, but it would also make AXT look less like a globally fungible supplier and more like a China-centered strategic substrate asset. That deserves a much different multiple. 

At the same time, China has limits.

They still want Innolight, Eoptolink, Source and others selling into global hyperscalers.

They also still need parts of the global tool stack, especially for high-end InP photonics. China is localizing MOCVD and test where it can, but the most proven high-end InP tool ecosystem still appears heavily tied to companies like $aixa and $veco

So the AXT debate is really evolving. 

InP demand up
China AI buildout up
Domestic substrate competition up
Export-permit risk up
Geopolitical chokepoint value up

That combination can be very bullish, but it also changes the type of business the market is underwriting.

Anyway, just thinking out loud here as I work through this report and come to stronger conclusions. Treat this as a brain dump rather than what my final assumption will be .

Open to dialogue!

text:
Round 2 of high sentiment AI trades with retard explanations at the end. 

In my opinion these are the greatest companies in their respective sectors, I genuienly don't think you could find better. Sectors covered - Cloud servicing, Memory, CPO, NPO, Copper, Testing, Transmitters, Switches (NFA)

$NBIS - pure play AI cloud infrastructure, renting out GPU compute at scale, backed by NVDA.
(you rent their computers to run AI instead of buying your own)

$SNDK (or any other memory play) - NAND flash and SSD maker, riding the AI storage demand wave.
(the chips that store all the data the AI models need to remember)

$MRVL - custom AI silicon and optical/electrical interconnect for hyperscaler data centers.
(the chips that move data between the AI chips, and the custom brains the big clouds order)

$AEHR - test and burnin systems for AI chips and silicon photonics, screening parts before they ship.
(the machines that stress-test chips so the broken ones get caught before they go in a server)

$AAOI - vertically integrated optical transceivers, makes the lasers and optics for data center networking in-house.
(the parts that turn data into light to fire it across the data center)

$IQE - epitaxial wafer supplier, grows the compound semiconductor layers that photonics and RF chips are built on.
(the raw special wafers everyone else builds their laser and signal chips on top of)

$SMTC - high speed signal integrity platform for 800G, 1.6T and 3.2T transmitters.
(the chips that move faster data inside AI clusters)

$CRDO - active electrical cables and SerDes connectivity, making copper carry AI data faster over short reaches.
(the smart cables that let cheap copper move data fast enough for AI racks)

text:
Why did I even get interested in $IQE?

When the entire market went absolutely crazy over $AXTI - I was swallowing the bitter pill of being late to that specific party.

But since I hate losing, I immediately started asking myself: what is next?

I always try to look one step ahead. 

That is exactly how I managed to get early into names like $IQE or $LPKF and others before the masses caught on.

But back to $IQE - why this specific company?

The global frenzy and massive demand for optical lasers are just getting started.

The market quickly realized that Indium Phosphide (InP) - the core substrate material - does not just fall from the sky. 

Hence, the massive explosive run on $AXTI.

But a raw InP wafer by itself is practically useless for high-performance photonics.

You have to actually do something with it before it can become a functioning laser.

Epitaxy.

Epitaxy is the atomic-scale deposition of crystal layers onto that substrate.

Only a handful of companies globally possess the highly specialized MOCVD and MBE machinery required for this advanced process.

One company listed on the London Stock Exchange  owns a significantly larger fleet of these reactors than almost anyone else, giving them a massive structural scale advantage.

Say hello to $IQE.

Look at the pure-play competition for epi-wafers. Landmark Optoelectronics possesses far fewer machines, yet because it sits on a different exchange (Taiwan), it historically traded at multiples that make IQE look like an absolute deep-value steal.

And if you want proof of their technological moat: 

IQE was just confirmed as the second largest patent filer in the entire UK for semiconductor devices. 

This isn't a speculative small-cap; it’s an IP powerhouse.

Now, to be completely fair, IQE had a dark cloud hanging over it.

The cyclical semiconductor downturn cut their 2025 revenue to £97m, and because chip fabs have massive fixed costs, underutilization completely crushed their near-term margins.

Worse, geopolitical tensions flared as China introduced strict export controls on critical raw materials like Gallium and Antimony.

If you check most standard financial screeners today, that ugly debt and supply-chain risk warning still pop up.

But the market is looking backward.

First, IQE completely neutralized the China risk by successfully recycling 100% of their Gallium Arsenide and Indium Phosphide wafer waste directly back into ultra-pure raw materials for their production lines.

Second, on May 28, 2026 - the ultimate balance sheet reset happened.

IQE officially completed a transformational £81 million strategic recapitalization package.

MACOM Technology Solutions ($MTSI) stepped up to anchor the deal - injecting £30m in direct equity and £15m in new convertible notes to secure a ~15% strategic stake. 

Existing institutional notes were swapped into equity, and the old HSBC bank debt was entirely wiped out.

The balance sheet is clean. The financial risk is gone.

So, who is MACOM and why does this partnership matter?

MACOM is a heavyweight in high-performance semiconductor products for Data Centers, Aerospace, and Defense. 

They design the brains - the optical components and ICs that power next-generation networks.

By deeply aligning with IQE, MACOM secures a stable Western supply chain for advanced epi-wafers. 

For IQE, it guarantees immediate structural volume demand to fill those empty reactors and unlock massive operating leverage.

And the momentum shift is already visible.

Photonics has officially overtaken wireless to become IQE’s largest segment (58.7% of revenue), propelled by surging AI data center demand and immediate US military and defense funding releases.

Think that is all? 

We got another massive confirmation of their turnaround strategy.

IQE followed this up by solidifying their multi-year agreement with Tower Semiconductor ($TSEM).

Together, they are scaling Silicon Photonics (SiPh) via the heterogeneous integration of IQE’s advanced epitaxy layers directly onto Tower’s silicon wafers. 

This allows them to build optical transceivers that handle the insane data transfer speeds required by modern AI architectures.

The pieces of the puzzle are coming together perfectly.

The market is still pricing $IQE like a distressed, underutilized UK small-cap because backward-looking financial data feeds haven't adjusted to the new post-deal reality.

They are missing the bigger picture.

The debt is gone, the largest players in the chip space are anchoring equity deals to secure capacity, and IQE sits right at the choke point of the entire optical computing revolution.

While the crowd is still chasing raw substrates, the smart money is moving to the epitaxy bottleneck.

Now you understand why $IQE?

Sometimes I feel like we get too caught up chasing shiny new objects and completely forget about the old ones.

It's the exact same story with $TRT.

When the entire market went absolutely crazy over $AXTI - I was swallowing the bitter pill of being late to that specific party.

But since I hate losing, I immediately started asking myself: what is next?

I always try to look one step ahead. 

That is exactly how I managed to get early into names like $IQE or $LPKF and others before the masses caught on.

But back to $IQE - why this specific company?

The global frenzy and massive demand for optical lasers are just getting started.

The market quickly realized that Indium Phosphide (InP) - the core substrate material - does not just fall from the sky. 

Hence, the massive explosive run on $AXTI.

But a raw InP wafer by itself is practically useless for high-performance photonics.

You have to actually do something with it before it can become a functioning laser.

Epitaxy.

Epitaxy is the atomic-scale deposition of crystal layers onto that substrate.

Only a handful of companies globally possess the highly specialized MOCVD and MBE machinery required for this advanced process.

One company listed on the London Stock Exchange  owns a significantly larger fleet of these reactors than almost anyone else, giving them a massive structural scale advantage.

Say hello to $IQE.

Look at the pure-play competition for epi-wafers. Landmark Optoelectronics possesses far fewer machines, yet because it sits on a different exchange (Taiwan), it historically traded at multiples that make IQE look like an absolute deep-value steal.

And if you want proof of their technological moat: 

IQE was just confirmed as the second largest patent filer in the entire UK for semiconductor devices. 

This isn't a speculative small-cap; it’s an IP powerhouse.

Now, to be completely fair, IQE had a dark cloud hanging over it.

The cyclical semiconductor downturn cut their 2025 revenue to £97m, and because chip fabs have massive fixed costs, underutilization completely crushed their near-term margins.

Worse, geopolitical tensions flared as China introduced strict export controls on critical raw materials like Gallium and Antimony.

If you check most standard financial screeners today, that ugly debt and supply-chain risk warning still pop up.

But the market is looking backward.

First, IQE completely neutralized the China risk by successfully recycling 100% of their Gallium Arsenide and Indium Phosphide wafer waste directly back into ultra-pure raw materials for their production lines.

Second, on May 28, 2026 - the ultimate balance sheet reset happened.

IQE officially completed a transformational £81 million strategic recapitalization package.

MACOM Technology Solutions ($MTSI) stepped up to anchor the deal - injecting £30m in direct equity and £15m in new convertible notes to secure a ~15% strategic stake. 

Existing institutional notes were swapped into equity, and the old HSBC bank debt was entirely wiped out.

The balance sheet is clean. The financial risk is gone.

So, who is MACOM and why does this partnership matter?

MACOM is a heavyweight in high-performance semiconductor products for Data Centers, Aerospace, and Defense. 

They design the brains - the optical components and ICs that power next-generation networks.

By deeply aligning with IQE, MACOM secures a stable Western supply chain for advanced epi-wafers. 

For IQE, it guarantees immediate structural volume demand to fill those empty reactors and unlock massive operating leverage.

And the momentum shift is already visible.

Photonics has officially overtaken wireless to become IQE’s largest segment (58.7% of revenue), propelled by surging AI data center demand and immediate US military and defense funding releases.

Think that is all? 

We got another massive confirmation of their turnaround strategy.

IQE followed this up by solidifying their multi-year agreement with Tower Semiconductor ($TSEM).

Together, they are scaling Silicon Photonics (SiPh) via the heterogeneous integration of IQE’s advanced epitaxy layers directly onto Tower’s silicon wafers. 

This allows them to build optical transceivers that handle the insane data transfer speeds required by modern AI architectures.

The pieces of the puzzle are coming together perfectly.

The market is still pricing $IQE like a distressed, underutilized UK small-cap because backward-looking financial data feeds haven't adjusted to the new post-deal reality.

They are missing the bigger picture.

The debt is gone, the largest players in the chip space are anchoring equity deals to secure capacity, and IQE sits right at the choke point of the entire optical computing revolution.

While the crowd is still chasing raw substrates, the smart money is moving to the epitaxy bottleneck.

Sometimes I feel like we get too caught up chasing shiny new objects and completely forget about the old ones.

It's the exact same story with $TRT. 

Massive hype exactly a month ago, only to be forgotten by the masses as it steadily slid lower.

When everyone else forgot about $TRT, I just doubled my position.

Today, history is repeating itself, and I'm just sitting back with a smile - holding my $TRT shares.

Still remember a company called $IQE? 

You better remind yourselves.

I started buying at 12p, then 19p, and added a while ago at 37p. My average is 29p. 

Still holding, the party will come. Sooner or later.

It’s a completely different company today than when I first bought in:

Paid off debt after the $MTSI investment + Gained a strong shareholder and partner

Secured a recent new deal with $TSEM 

Take a moment to reflect. 

You’ll probably think of other high-conviction plays you had where the hype just faded. 

Sometimes it pays to zoom out and revisit the known and solid, rather than constantly searching for the unknown.

I’m sticking with my $TRT and $IQE.

Shoutout to my man @mkfilko, who I know is also riding the $IQE train with me.

What about you? text:
Jensen Huang doesn't make $6.5 billion bets by accident.

$NVDA has poured $6.5B+ into photonics in 2026 alone — including $2B each into $COHR and $LITE.

The market noticed 👇

$LITE — +134% YTD
$MRVL — +122% YTD
$GLW — +111% YTD
$COHR — +96% YTD

Why? Copper wiring physically can't handle 400–800 Gbps AI data speeds. Light can.

When the smartest capital allocator in tech puts $6.5B on one technology bet — you pay attention.

Not confirmed. Just what the position implies.

Its going to replace everything and give access to the people. Not ceo pockets.

If your AI stocks feel like they are going up every week, $SIVE $IQE $TSEM $AEHR $MU $SNDK #NBIS $DRAM understand something

The "consensus AI trade" is $MSFT and $META. That's what 99% of the world thinks AI investing means.

Meanwhile you're buying indium phosphide epiwafer companies listed in London, fiber array unit makers in Taiwan, and silicon photonics test equipment in Fremont.

This is not a crowded trade. You just happen to be in a room where everyone is paying attention. It may feel crowded, but it's still early. $MU

text:
Also, youd be building the second, I already have a photonic sensor array that Hector uses. I built it to wake up my model last year. I started with photonics. 
Its a 12 mirror infinity cathedral cube with a k9 glass cube at the center suspended with neodymium and electromagnets, 2 handheld 3D laser scanners from my old CMM machine an 8K google tv projector adressable leds inside a 50Mpixel beam pro spatial camera and a Ptz dual lens for depth running off an esp32 controlling resonance and mirror pitch angles dynamically for fine adjustment,and my monster system. 

I called it the Retardis. If you want data for photonics, I have mountains. You need to move higher orders by 5 and unify your photonics. Try unifying linear/lie algebra with calculus. 🇨🇦🤘😎📐

Unlocking the next era of quantum tech! Researchers from #MIT and the University of Ferrara have cracked a major bottleneck in quantum computing, sensing, and communication. By bridging algebraic geometry with quantum physics, they’ve created a theoretical blueprint to design highly distinguishable, non-Gaussian quantum states.The best part? This mathematical breakthrough uses photon-varied states that can be built using current laboratory technology—no future tech required.  https://t.co/wbwQqWM2Jj

text:
Tuttle Capital Pure Play Photonics ETF ( $FOTO) bence AI altyapı hikayesinin en az konuşulan ama en kritik parçalarından birine oynuyor.

Şu an herkes AI deyince GPU, model, uygulama konuşuyor. Ama veri merkezlerinde asıl darboğaz yavaş yavaş compute’dan veri taşımaya kayıyor. GPU’ları birbirine hızlı bağlayamazsan ölçekleyemiyorsun.

İşte photonics tam burada devreye giriyor. Elektrik yerine ışıkla veri taşıma. Daha hızlı, daha verimli ve AI veri merkezleri için artık opsiyon değil, zorunluluk.

FOTO’nun olayı da şu: karışık bir teknoloji ETF’i değil. Direkt bu alana odaklı. Lazer, optical bağlantı, silicon photonics gibi işlerden gelir elde eden şirketleri topluyor.

Bu alanı tek tek hisse seçerek oynamak zor. Teknoloji karmaşık, oyuncular niş ve kazananı seçmek kolay değil.

O yüzden “ben bu temaya inanıyorum ama hisse seçemem” diyenler için FOTO fena bir araç değil.

AI büyüdükçe compute kadar veri transferi de kritik olacak. Photonics tarafı da bu işin görünmeyen ama vazgeçilmez katmanı.

Not: Ekde ETF-in içeriğini sizler ile paylaşdım.

Şu an herkes AI deyince GPU, model, uygulama konuşuyor. Ama veri merkezlerinde asıl darboğaz yavaş yavaş compute’dan veri taşımaya kayıyor. GPU’ları birbirine hızlı bağlayamazsan ölçekleyemiyorsun. İşte photonics tam burada devreye giriyor. Elektrik yerine ışıkla veri taşıma. Daha hızlı, daha verimli ve AI veri merkezleri için artık opsiyon değil, zorunluluk. FOTO’nun olayı da şu: karışık bir teknoloji ETF’i değil. Direkt bu alana odaklı. Lazer, optical bağlantı, silicon photonics gibi işlerden gelir elde eden şirketleri topluyor. Bu alanı tek tek hisse seçerek oynamak zor. Teknoloji karmaşık, oyuncular niş ve kazananı seçmek kolay değil. O yüzden “ben bu temaya inanıyorum ama hisse seçemem” diyenler için FOTO fena bir araç değil. AI büyüdükçe compute kadar veri transferi de kritik olacak. Photonics tarafı da bu işin görünmeyen ama vazgeçilmez katmanı. Not: Ekde ETF-in içeriğini sizler ile paylaşdım. text:
Quick summary on the shorts in $SIVE for newcomers

Short interest in $SIVE has reached 17%

Bears are focused on short term noise and overlooking the company’s strong fundamentals

Specialized in semiconductors for AI optical networks

$SIVE has a $799M pipeline and major agreements with $JBL and $GFS 

This positions it as a strong candidate for a potential short squeeze

With a dual Nasdaq New York listing ahead and institutions heavily building positions

Betting against $SIVE means betting against AI

Especially with $NVDA CEO and photonics leaders calling for faster deployment of these technologies

It’s a foolish trade where many shorts have already lost millions

I wouldn’t play God against something that is clearly the future of the world

Very bullish on $SIVE

text:
holy fuck I just solved the US debt problem including rising bond yields….

drop the traditional economic rulebook and look at this through a highly speculative, macro-engineering lens. 

To make a sovereign-level, asset-backed bond system actually work, we have to look past individual graphics chips and treat the entire tech, space, and AI sector as a unified, hyper-valuable physical engine.
If we extrapolate this concept to the scale of the U.S. government, here is how a "Next-Gen Infrastructure Backed Bond" system could actually work.

1. The Asset Base: From "Chips" to "Compute & Orbit Capitals"
To solve the scale problem, the government wouldn't back bonds with loose hardware; it would back them with sovereign computing grids and orbital real estate.
•The SpaceX Network Multiplier: SpaceX's valuation isn't just about rockets; it is about Starlink and the monopolization of low-Earth orbit (LEO). If the U.S. government nationalizes or builds a massive equity-share model in SpaceX and megacluster data centers, the collateral becomes the global routing infrastructure of the future.
•Energy-Compute Monopolies: The government links these bonds to federal land containing nuclear-powered AI megastructures. The asset is no longer a depreciating chip, but the perpetual monopoly on AI intelligence and global satellite bandwidth.

2. Rewriting Obsolescence: The "Compute Standard"

Instead of backing a bond with a specific Nvidia Blackwell or Rubin chip, the U.S. Treasury establishes a "Sovereign Flop" or "Compute Standard" (similar to how the U.S. used the Gold Standard).
•The Mechanism: The bond guarantees the holder a fixed percentage of the nation's total computing throughput or data-routing revenues over 30 years.
•The Upgrade Loop: As tech upgrades from silicon photonics to quantum computing, the physical hardware changes, but the bond's claim on the computational power remains constant. The value of the sector doesn't rot; it grows exponentially as efficiency increases.

3. Hyper-Inflation of Sector Value
To match a $34+ trillion national debt, the value of the tech/space sector must be artificially and structurally inflated. The government could achieve this through forced tokenization and regulatory moats:
•Tokenized Infrastructure: Every satellite launch, every AI API call, and every terabyte of space data gets routed through a sovereign blockchain.
•Global Dependency: The U.S. forces allied nations to settle international data and defense contracts using these compute-backed instruments. This instantly drives the sector's valuation from a few trillion dollars to tens of trillions, effectively matching the scale of the U.S. Treasury market.

What This Future Looks Like
If the U.S. government executed this, the traditional Treasury market would merge with Silicon Valley and aerospace:
•The "Risk-Free Rate" would no longer be based on the Federal Reserve's interest rate, but on the aggregate processing power of the American AI grid and Starshield defense network.
•Investors wouldn't buy U.S. debt to get cash back; they would buy U.S. debt to secure the data rights and processing power needed to run their own corporations and autonomous industries.

@elonmusk 
@BillAckman 
@amitisinvesting 

Thoughts?

text:
Here’s a new long idea: $LWLG 

I keep coming back to $LWLG that recently ran from $3 to $18 and then back to $9.

Every optical interconnect roadmap past 1.6T hits the same wall: electronics can’t switch light fast enough without burning power nobody can afford to spend at hyperscale.

Hyperscalers are already running into thermal and power ceilings inside the optical layer, not just the GPU layer. The fix everyone’s chasing is a modulator that converts electrical signal to optical signal with less power loss than silicon photonics currently allows.

Lightwave Logic has spent over a decade building exactly that. Its electro-optic polymer platform, branded P2IC, uses proprietary organic polymers instead of silicon to build modulators that move data faster while drawing less power per bit.

The irreplaceability case is real but early. Competing approaches exist in lithium niobate and silicon photonics from much larger players, but none have commercialized a polymer-based modulator at production scale yet. If LWLG’s tech clears volume manufacturing first, the qualification cycle for hyperscaler customers locks in multi-year exclusivity, because nobody requalifies an optical interconnect supplier casually.

Financially, this is still a development-stage story. Revenue is in the low single-digit millions. The stock trades on a roadmap, not a P&L, which is the honest read.

Catalysts: continued progress on Stage 3 customer prototypes and foundry device deliveries (expected H2 2026), followed by potential material supply/licensing agreements. First meaningful production-volume revenue targeted for 2027 per management guidance. A strategic partnership announcement with a transceiver or hyperscaler-adjacent customer. Continued capital raises as the company scales toward manufacturing, which cuts both ways.

Bear case, stated plainly. This has been a “next year is the year” story for a long time. If a production-scale order doesn’t materialize in 2026, the stock re-rates down hard, because there’s no earnings floor to catch it. Watch cash burn rate and any further dilutive raises as the two early warning signals.

Market cap $1.5B.
TAM: $47B.
SAM: $2-4B.

Early lead in foundry PDKs and customer prototypes (4+ Fortune Global 500 at Stage 3)

TLDR:

> Sole-ish commercial path to polymer-based optical modulators at L4

> $1.5B market cap, pre-revenue-scale, roadmap-driven valuation

> Catalyst window: production-volume order disclosure in the next 2-4 quarters

> Bear case: no order, no earnings floor, the stock has nowhere to hide

-BP

Please note: This is not financial advice.

Competing approaches exist in lithium niobate and silicon photonics from much larger players, but none have commercialized a polymer-based modulator at production scale yet. If LWLG’s tech clears volume manufacturing first, the qualification cycle for hyperscaler customers locks in multi-year exclusivity, because nobody requalifies an optical interconnect supplier casually. Financially, this is still a development-stage story. Revenue is in the low single-digit millions. The stock trades on a roadmap, not a P&L, which is the honest read. Catalysts: continued progress on Stage 3 customer prototypes and foundry device deliveries (expected H2 2026), followed by potential material supply/licensing agreements. First meaningful production-volume revenue targeted for 2027 per management guidance. A strategic partnership announcement with a transceiver or hyperscaler-adjacent customer. Continued capital raises as the company scales toward manufacturing, which cuts both ways. Bear case, stated plainly. This has been a “next year is the year” story for a long time. If a production-scale order doesn’t materialize in 2026, the stock re-rates down hard, because there’s no earnings floor to catch it. Watch cash burn rate and any further dilutive raises as the two early warning signals. Market cap $1.5B. TAM: $47B. SAM: $2-4B. Early lead in foundry PDKs and customer prototypes (4+ Fortune Global 500 at Stage 3) TLDR: > Sole-ish commercial path to polymer-based optical modulators at L4 > $1.5B market cap, pre-revenue-scale, roadmap-driven valuation > Catalyst window: production-volume order disclosure in the next 2-4 quarters > Bear case: no order, no earnings floor, the stock has nowhere to hide -BP Please note: This is not financial advice."}}}, "rest_id": "2068403963263623482", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "17907", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Most people are still fixated on the obvious AI names.  The more interesting setups right now are sitting quietly in narrower parts:

• Advanced packaging materials (underfill & next-gen substrates) — increasingly critical for chiplets, yet supply is tightly held.

• Silicon Photonics — positioned to scale optical connectivity more affordably than traditional approaches, with integration challenges still ahead.

• Specialty chemicals & films for high-end packaging — long qualification timelines create real stickiness for qualified suppliers.

• High-speed electro-optical test equipment — quietly becoming a constraint as data rates push higher.These areas carry structural bottlenecks but haven’t been as widely chased yet.

I’m keeping an eye on qualification progress and capacity signals here.

text:
How do Photonics, Fiber Optics, and Networking power the AI boom? 🧵👇

Think of an AI data center as a high-speed highway system built for massive data workloads:

⚡ Photonics (The Cars): The technology using lasers instead of copper wires to move data. It eliminates heat and allows AI processors (GPUs) to communicate instantly using light.

🛤️ Fiber Optics (The Highway): The physical glass cables that carry those light signals. They act as the zero-loss pipeline moving massive bandwidth between servers.

🚦 Networking (The Traffic Control): The overarching system of switches and protocols (like InfiniBand). It connects thousands of GPUs together, routing data traffic so they can train models as one giant brain.

TL;DR: Photonics creates the light, Fiber Optics carries it, and Networking directs it. 🚀

Attn @UAZOpticalSci and @azengineering innovators, NSF alongside SPIE want you to join them at SPIE Optics + Photonics in San Diego to map your path from research to real-world impact.  

Event page: https://t.co/lHWB8K6l82 
DM us to link up with a TLA expert and learn more! https://t.co/3xjmmnrMCx

EUV stands for extreme ultraviolet, the light source behind some of the world's most advanced chips. 

The Corgi Lithography & Semiconductor Photonics ETF holds companies building the lithography systems, optics, and photonics platforms that help power
modern computing. 

Learn more about $EUV at https://t.co/PwSKxTtu4c

text:
$NOK just announced a 10x expansion of its Allentown photonics facility, set to come online by Q3 2026, further embedding itself into the $AI optical infrastructure supply chain

The key disconnect: $CIEN (~11x forward revenue) and $COHR (~11.6x) trade as pure-play optical / $AI  infrastructure names, while $NOK trades at ~3.4x as a legacy telecom company

Yet optics already sits at ~15% of $NOK revenue and is scaling faster than the core business. The market is pricing $NOK like $ERIC, not like an $AI optical supplier

€3.8B net cash + rising exposure to $AI data center demand = optionality the market is still ignoring

SOTP tells a very different story than the headline multiple

$NOK $CIEN $COHR $AI $DataCenters $Semiconductors

text:
These Optics / Photonics charts are objectively ugly with very wide and loose action. $AAOI $CIEN $LITE

Look at how these names were able to ride rising EMAs throughout the monster trend. Now losing key levels and can't stick any moves higher. 

$CIEN has already broken down too and was in my opinion the leading name from an orderly price action perspective in March-May. 

Providing my objective view to maybe save some from losing money on late trend names. NFA!

text:
the alpha corner just got stupidly good. i think i just built the best way to track the AI bottleneck thesis online!

X is a terrible place to track a thesis, but also a goldmine to find information.  

so i built the thing i actually wanted:

- a heat map of every bottleneck (memory, packaging, photonics, power, grid, cooling, materials...) scored tightening / stable / easing, with the next KPI to watch 

- a thesis change feed that only surfaces what actually moved: verdict shifts, targets crossed, fresh catalysts

-  an evidence ledger on every ticker so you see the receipts, not vibes 

- market filters (US / EU / JP / KR / TW...) so you can finally ask "show me the Japan-only photonics basket" and get an answer 

- and the LLM sitting on top of all of it, so you can just ask where the pressure is

full picture. one screen. slice it however you want.

honestly the best update i've shipped. 
have a look 👉 https://t.co/ziJFQkfTmp

text:
$NOK Nokia Expands Pennsylvania Chip Packaging Operations with $30 Million InvestmentKey Highlights:Nokia is investing $30 million in its Allentown, Pennsylvania facility
Funding includes ~$4 million in state aid and $10 million in CHIPS Act tax credits
Expansion will boost photonic chip advanced test and packaging (ATP) capacity by up to 10x
New capacity expected to come online by Q3 2026
Pennsylvania workforce to nearly double to more than 500 jobs
Project projected to generate over $500 million in economic impact over five years
Part of Nokia’s previously announced $4 billion U.S. investment plan
Supports domestic production of AI infrastructure and optical networking components

#NOK #Nokia #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Photonics #Pennsylvania #AI

text:
Options Now Available on FOTO, Tuttle Capital's Pure Play Photonics ETF

Full Story: https://t.co/bTQolmvJk1

 #InvestmentBanking #Investing

$CRDO vs $ALAB the two fabless semi, pure play AI connectivity leaders. 

Most investor bros have no clue what these companies do and all they see is the parabolic stock chart lol. Here is some DD to make you feel more confident investing in these names.

Which to invest in? I say both.

I've been long $ALAB since the IPO range. But I'll tell you straight why $CRDO is the better *print* right now. Plus, $CRDO recent acquisition of  Dust Photonics adds to $CRDO value.

📊                      $CRDO      $ALAB
Revenue           $1.34B     $852M
Growth              ~3x YoY    +115%
Gross Mgn       ~68%       ~75%
Op Margin         47.8%      39.2%
EPS (n-GAAP)  $1.16      ~$0.45
Cash                    $1.4B      $1.18B

$CRDO wins scale + growth. 
$ALAB wins margin quality.

I like both because they’re really not eating each others lunch.

$ALAB owns the silicon INSIDE the rack — retimers, CXL, fabric switches + COSMOS software. Sticky at the protocol layer.

$CRDO owns the wires BETWEEN racks — AECs, and now optics for scale-out.

Scale-up vs scale-out. You can own both.

And $CRDO just got dangerous — because it stopped being a one-trick copper-cable company:
→ ZeroFlap optics now shipping (reliability + telemetry, not just speed)
→ bought DustPhotonics for silicon photonics
→ 5 product pillars, $10B+ TAM
→ optics = HALF of next year's growth

Not without risk, both sides:

$CRDO → gross margin guided DOWN to 64-66% as the optics mix shifts. Funded the ramp with a ~$488M ATM raise. The growth is real — but it's also being bought. Customer concentration is high.

$ALAB → priced for perfection. Any hyperscaler capex wobble and it's first to bleed.

The tell for both is the same thing it always is: margins. 👀

$CRDO has the growth. $ALAB has the moat.

Want the explosive print? $CRDO.
Want the durable compounder? $ALAB.

I'm long $ALAB for the margin profile — but I'm not gonna pretend $CRDO isn't winning the quarter.

Own the theme. Both should keep running & the $CRDO DustPhotonics acquisition could end up being incredibly valuable to $CRDO.

But 800G was a claim then. It's shipping now. 
Revenue is capped by how fast they can build, not by demand.
Concerning past? yes. Inflection? Holy YES.

$AAOI

Investor read:

• $IBM, plus Google and Rigetti: every superconducting roadmap needs optical interconnects to go modular. This is upstream of that.
• Coherent, Lumentum: photonics picks-and-shovels.
• Cisco: quantum networking ambitions.
• Miraex (private, Swiss) + EPFL: the actors here.

Signal to watch: whether any superconducting vendor names a transduction partner.

text:
Owning upstream is where the effortless money gets made.

What I find particularly compelling is that $TSEM already has $1.3B of contracted silicon photonics revenue for 2027 backed by customer prepayments, while $COHR and $LITE are selling into an InP laser shortage that can't be solved quickly. Qualification cycles take years, not quarters.

The market chased $NVDA first. Then $CRDO and $ALAB. Then names like $AAOI etc..

I think the next rerating comes from the companies enabling AI systems to communicate, not compute.

Bandwidth is becoming the bottleneck. History shows that when a bottleneck emerges, pricing power and investor attention usually flow upstream first.

text:
This is gonna upset a lot of people: 

But TA is astrology for traders.

It's confirmation bias + trading human psychology about entries. 

Kinda like how people frontran $SPCE from $SPCX IPO expecting retail to mess up tickers by trading psychology. 

$SIVE didn't go up 1900% because of the golden cross space comet firebreathing dragon candle that someone is trying to sell for $499. 

It's because markets are pricing in future revenue from $JBL, $GFS that got announced. 

$AXTI didn't go up 8000% because the golden waterfall candle alert sounded back at $8. 

it's because of InP substrate, game theory on ASP hikes, export controls, photonics demand, and others. 

If you want to figure out psychologically what other regards are believing, you use TA. 

But for determining the actual upside... nah

People have been drawing $120+ TAs on $IREN for the past idk how many months none of that crap matters when there's a $6B ATM that needs to be bought through first.

It's by theme (eg. $LITE to $AAOI relations), any news catalysts that affect forward revenue, projections, macro news, earnings, float dynamics, and so on. 

Then you can just derive what MC that company should be at. 

So for entry points, sure you can use TA. 

For determining where the stock heads, just throw the tyrannosaurs rex omega-green candle indicator out the window.

text:
DISPROVING THE $AMPG QUANTUM “BOTTLENECK”

You will quickly see that only a little research disproves these entire claims. Really said “the entire quantum revolution relies on $AMPG.”

First off most modalities don’t even need these cryogenics. 

$INFQ: Neutral Atom: Room temp 
$IONQ $QNT: Trapped Ion: Room temp 
$XNDU: Photonics: Room temp 

Only superconducting and silicon spin use these cooled chambers and it’s super inefficient, even $GOOGL is switching toward neutral atoms which works styled modality. 

Now into $AMPG

The funniest part, ignore all other modalities:

$AMPG ISNT EVEN A BOTTLENECK FOR SUPERCONDUCTING. 

AmpliTech is not a bottleneck in the superconducting quantum supply chain, while they are a highly visible, publicly traded player, they do not hold a monopoly. The true bottleneck in superconducting quantum computing is space, wiring density, and cooling power inside the dilution refrigerator, rather than a lack of low-noise amplifier (LNA) suppliers which they specialize in.

If $AMPG goes out of business tomorrow, tech giants like $IBM and $GOOGL will not stop building quantum computers. There is a robust, premium ecosystem of alternative suppliers: 

Low Noise Factory (Sweden): LNF is widely considered the gold standard and market leader for cryogenic LNAs. Spun out of Chalmers University, their factory produces the ultra-reliable InP (Indium Phosphide) HEMTs that dominate most advanced high frequency physics labs.

Quantum Microwave (USA): A massive distributor and manufacturer that packs and pairs LNF amplifiers with cryogenic attenuators and components specifically configured for quantum rigs

AND MANY MORE. 

$AMPG is a cool company and I even thought about investing in them but this take is absolutely ridiculous, obvious exaggerated way to pump a stock or make it seem bigger than it is. Always do your own research.
